steps.prompt: Which word is a finite verb form?

hints.timere: 'timere' is the present infinitive (to fear) — it completes the construction but is not finite
hints.noli: 'noli' is the singular imperative of 'nolle' — 'Nolite' is the plural form used here
hints.timore: 'timore' is the ablative of 'timor' (fear, the noun) — not present in this sentence

steps.target: to be unwilling / to not wish
choices: to be unwilling / to not wish

hints.to harm: 'to harm' is 'nocere' — 'Nolite' comes from 'nolle' (to be unwilling)

hints.Indicative: Indicative would state a fact — 'Nolite' is a command (do not!)
hints.Subjunctive: Subjunctive would express wish or possibility — 'Nolite' is a direct prohibition
hints.Infinitive: Infinitive would be 'nolle' — 'Nolite' is the 2nd person plural imperative

hints.volo, velle, volui, —: These are the parts of 'velle' (to wish) — 'nolle' is its negative (non + velle)
hints.nosco, noscere, novi, notum: These are the parts of 'noscere' (to learn/know)
hints.noceo, nocere, nocui, nocitum: These are the parts of 'nocere' (to harm)

hints.Nolite: 'Nolite' is a plural imperative, not an infinitive
hints.timor: 'timor' is the noun 'fear' — not present in this phrase
hints.timebat: 'timebat' is an imperfect indicative form — not present in this phrase

hints.to time: Latin 'timere' has no relation to English 'time' — it means 'to fear'

hints.Purpose clause: A purpose clause uses 'ut/ne' + subjunctive — 'Nolite + infinitive' is a prohibition
hints.Indirect statement: Indirect statement uses accusative + infinitive — this is a direct command
hints.Result clause: A result clause uses 'ut' + subjunctive — 'Nolite + infinitive' forms a negative command

hints.teneo, tenere, tenui, tentum: These are the parts of 'tenere' (to hold)
hints.moneo, monere, monui, monitum: These are the parts of 'monere' (to warn)
hints.habeo, habere, habui, habitum: These are the parts of 'habere' (to have)
